danger_signals.visual_form: Character sprite falls to ground and remains prone
danger_signals.meaning: Player eliminated from play for remainder of half; repeated tackling causes this state

danger_signals.visual_form: Ball sprite control shifts to CPU-controlled players
danger_signals.meaning: Immediate threat to goal; opponent can execute super shots

danger_signals.visual_form: Bicycle kick animation or diving header animation triggers
danger_signals.meaning: High-damage attack incoming; team-specific super shots vary in trajectory and power

safety_signals.visual_form: Character sprite in active playing pose
safety_signals.meaning: Player available for control and tactical positioning
Offensive opportunity; can execute pass/shoot commands
Defensive protection active; last barrier against scoring

faction_indicators.color_identity: Palette-swapped team coloring (specific hue varies by port)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Player sprites recolored to represent national team; same base character models as other teams

faction_indicators.color_identity: Distinct palette swap identifying national team
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Sprite palette differentiation from other teams

faction_indicators.color_identity: Sprite palette for Japanese national team
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Kunio character reused from Downtown Nekketsu Story/River City Ransom
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Shared sprite assets with Kunio-kun series; recognizable character faces from series canon

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Playing field rendered with ice texture; white/crystalline ground appearance
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player and ball movement physics altered; reduced traction creates sliding, slower acceleration, altered ball bounce

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Sand texture applied to playing field; tan/brown ground material
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Movement physics adjusted to sand resistance; affects player speed and ball control

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Dirt texture on playing field; earthy brown ground appearance
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Standard movement physics with terrain-specific ball behavior

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Super shot count resets from five to five; knocked-down players reset to standing
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Eliminated players return to field; team recovers one-half resource pool

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Team palette and super shot animation changes reflect advancing opponent nationalities
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: CPU opponent difficulty/behavior patterns shift based on tournament stage; different super shot characteristics encountered

environmental_storytelling.example: Ice, sand, and dirt fields serve as mechanical storytelling—environmental context defines play challenge without narrative exposition

Kunio and Japanese team reuse sprites from Downtown Nekketsu Story series, embedding narrative continuity through visual recognition; short-legged big-headed character archetype signals Kunio-kun series identity
Thirteen distinct national teams communicated entirely through sprite color differentiation and super shot animation variations; no text-based faction markers required
Each team's unique super shot animation style communicates national character and playstyle without mechanical explanation; visual signature of team identity

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Positioned in goal area; intercepts shots; default defensive stance
Falls prone after repeated tackles; remains inactive for remainder of half; does not participate in play
Executes bicycle kick or diving header animation; generates high-powered shot with team-specific trajectory
Slides with reduced friction; travels farther; maintains momentum longer
Receives pass/shoot command input; can execute five super shots per half
Executes team-specific super shot patterns; varies difficulty based on tournament progression

notes: Nintendo World Cup (1990) communicates world-state and faction identity primarily through visual palette swapping and environmental ground textures. The game lacks explicit narrative text or symbolic markers; all faction differentiation derives from sprite color recoloring. The Japanese version (Nekketsu High School Dodgeball Club: Soccer Story) centers on intra-school competition and reuses character assets from the Kunio-kun series, embedding narrative continuity through sprite recognition. The international NES localization transforms this into a 13-nation world cup by palette-swapping identical base player models. Field material (ice/sand/dirt) serves dual function: mechanical differentiation affecting gameplay physics and environmental storytelling establishing context. Super shot animations provide team-specific visual signatures communicating playstyle identity without text exposition. No explicit danger/safety UI elements documented; gameplay threats communicated through sprite state changes (knockdown, possession shift) and animation triggers (super shot charge).
